%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Simple Plotting

> **Section Objective:** Learn the core MATLAB plotting workflow — plot data, label axes, add a title, and choose the right style for your data type.

Imagine you have already computed two vectors, `t` and `f`, holding 500 sampled values each. Reading those numbers tells you nothing at a glance. Plotting them instantly reveals the signal's shape. This section teaches the essential MATLAB plotting workflow: call `plot`, label the axes, optionally add a title, and choose the right visual style for your data. Two textbook examples anchor the ideas — a sinusoid drawn as a smooth connected curve, and complex roots displayed as separate point markers. Master these patterns and you will be able to produce a correct, readable plot quickly under exam conditions without hesitation.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The plot command takes two vectors of equal length: the first supplies horizontal coordinates, the second supplies vertical coordinates. The result is a graph of paired sample points.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214561854-6487.png)

## 1. Basic plot Command

The command `plot(t,f)` tells MATLAB to treat each entry of `t` as a horizontal coordinate and the matching entry of `f` as the corresponding vertical coordinate. Because `t` and `f` are vectors of sampled values, the plot is built from many coordinate pairs joined into a curve.

### TEXTBOOK EXAMPLE

Suppose `t` holds 500 equally spaced points over the interval \(0 \leq t < 0.2\) seconds, and `f` stores the sampled values of \(f(t) = \sin(2\pi \cdot 10t + \pi/6)\). Calling `plot(t,f)` draws exactly two complete cycles of the sinusoid across that interval — one cycle every 0.1 seconds.

### EXAM TIP

If your graph looks wrong, the first thing to check is whether `t` and `f` correspond entry-by-entry. A mismatch in vector length or a misaligned index is the most common source of a garbled plot. Fix the vectors before suspecting the formula.

$$f(t) = \sin(2\pi \cdot 10t + \pi/6)$$
*This is the sinusoid being sampled into the vector `f` and then visualized with `plot(t,f)`, so the formula and the resulting graph are two representations of the same signal — one algebraic, one visual.*

## 2. Making a Plot Readable

A graph without labels is incomplete. After calling `plot(t,f)`, add axis labels immediately:

```matlab
xlabel('t');
ylabel('f(t)');
title('Sinusoid over 0.2 seconds');
```

Labels are not decoration — they tell the reader what each axis represents and make your answer defensible in reports, labs, and exams. The `title` command adds a heading above the plot when the context needs one.

### DISCRETE DATA: USE MARKERS

For data that consists of separate, isolated values rather than a continuous signal, connect the points with a style string instead of a line. The textbook example uses the 100 unique roots of \(w^{100} = -1\): each root is a distinct complex number, so the command

```matlab
plot(real(w), imag(w), 'o')
```

places a circular marker at each root's location in the complex plane without drawing connecting lines between them.

### KEY CONTRAST

| Data type | Recommended style |
|-----------|------------------|
| Continuous signal (sinusoid) | `plot(t,f)` — connected curve |
| Discrete solutions or roots | `plot(x,y,'o')` — point markers |

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Left: a continuous sinusoid plotted as a connected curve with plot(t,f). Right: discrete complex roots plotted as separate markers with plot(real(w),imag(w),'o'). The warning box flags the axis equal requirement for geometric accuracy.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214723431-9368.png)

## 3. Axis Equal and the Common Geometry Trap

When you plot real versus imaginary parts of complex numbers, the horizontal and vertical axes must use the same scale if you want the shape to be visually trustworthy. By default, MATLAB stretches each axis independently to fill the figure window.

The fix is one command:

```matlab
axis equal
```

This forces one unit on the horizontal axis to occupy the same pixel length as one unit on the vertical axis.

### WHY IT MATTERS

The textbook example computes the 100 unique roots of \(w^{100} = -1\). Those roots lie equally spaced on the unit circle — but without `axis equal`, the plot may look like an ellipse even though every computed root is mathematically correct.

### PATTERN RECOGNITION

If a supposed circle looks stretched or squashed, suspect axis scaling before doubting your math. Add `axis equal` and check again.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- `plot(x,y)` uses the first vector as horizontal coordinates and the second as vertical coordinates.
- Add `xlabel`, `ylabel`, and `title` so every axis is labeled and the plot is exam-ready.
- Use a marker string like `'o'` for discrete points, and `axis equal` when plotting circular geometric data.

*In the next section we will use plotting together with element-by-element operations to compare related signals.*
